sections.heading: Linking
sections.body: When a word ends in a consonant and the next begins with a vowel, they link together as if part of the same word: 'an apple' → /ə ˈnæp.əl/, 'turn it off' → /ˈtɜr.nɪt ɑf/. This is the most frequent connected speech process.

sections.heading: Elision
sections.body: Elision is the disappearance of a sound. The most common: /t/ and /d/ between two consonants ('last night' → /læs naɪt/, 'next door' → /nɛks dɔr/), and the /h/ in unstressed pronouns ('tell him' → /ˈtɛl ɪm/).

sections.heading: Assimilation
sections.body: Assimilation is when one sound changes to become more like an adjacent sound. 'Ten boys' → /ˈtem bɔɪz/ (n → m before a bilabial). 'That person' → /ˈðæp ˈpɜrsən/ (t → p before bilabial). This happens automatically in fast speech.

exercises.instruction: Identify which process (linking, elision, or assimilation) applies to each phrase.

quiz.question: In 'that person', the /t/ at the end of 'that' changes to /p/ because:
options: Assimilation: the /t/ anticipates the bilabial /p/ that follows
quiz.explanation: This is anticipatory (regressive) assimilation — the place of articulation of /t/ shifts to match the following /p/.
